1:
מִזְמוֹר לְדָוִד (mizmôr lĕdāwid, "A Psalm of David") — mizmôr is from zāmar (to sing with string accompaniment, to pluck/play), not a generic word for poem. It specifically indicates a song meant for instruments. The superscription situates the psalm in the aftermath of Absalom's rebellion (2 Sam 15-18), giving the corporate lament an autobiographical anchor.

רַבּוּ צָרָי (rabbû ṣārāy, "how many are my foes!") — the opening exclamation without a particle: the proliferation of enemies is not calmly observed but blurted out. Ṣārāy (my adversaries) from ṣārar: to press tight, bind, besiege — not casual opponents but those who press hard against him.
2: אֵין יְשׁוּעָתָה לּוֹ בֵאלֹהִים (ʾên yĕshûʿātāh llô bēlōhîm, "there is no salvation for him in God") — the enemies' taunt is theological: they claim that even God is not able or willing to rescue. The final particle selāh (סֶלָה) appears here for the first time in the Psalter; its precise meaning is uncertain (a musical interlude? a pause for reflection? from sālal, to lift up), but it marks a break after which the Psalmist turns to address God directly.
3: מָגֵן בַּעֲדִי (māgên baʿădî, "a shield around me") — māgên is the small handheld shield (vs. ṣinnāh, the full-body shield of Psalms 5:12). God as personal protection surrounding the Psalmist on all sides (baʿădî = "about me/on my behalf"). מֵרִים רֹאשִׁי (mêrîm rōʾshî, "the one who lifts my head") — the participle indicates ongoing action: God is continuously the one lifting the head, a gesture of honor, dignity, and courage in contrast to the enemies' contempt.
4: קוֹלִי אֶל-יְהוָה אֶקְרָא (qôlî ʾel-YHWH ʾeqrāʾ, "I cried out to the LORD") — the imperfect (ʾeqrāʾ) here functions as a future-historical narration: "I will call/I called." מֵהַר קָדְשׁוֹ (mêhar qodshô, "from his holy hill") — the answer comes from Zion, the place of the divine dwelling, reinforcing that the LORD who installs the King (Psalms 2:6) also hears from that same place.
5: שָׁכַבְתִּי וָאִישָׁנָה (shākabetî wāʾîshānāh, "I lay down and slept") — both verbs are in the perfect (completed action): David actually slept. The waw-consecutive pattern (wāʾîshānāh) presents the actions as sequential narrative. In the middle of violent crisis — enemies surrounding him — he slept. הֱקִיצוֹתִי (hĕqîṣôtî, "I awoke") — the act of waking is attributed to divine sustaining: "the LORD sustained me." Sleep and waking alike are acts of trust, not statements of invulnerability.
6: לֹא-אִירָא (lōʾ-ʾîrāʾ, "I will not fear") — imperfect negated: ongoing, habitual non-fear. The tens of thousands (רִבְבוֹת, rĕbābôt) surrounding on every side (שָׁתוּ עָלַי סָבִיב) represent the most extreme situation imaginable — total encirclement by vast numbers. The confidence of v5-6 flows from the fact of v4: God heard and answered.
7: קוּמָה יְהוָה (qûmāh YHWH, "Rise up, O LORD!") — the imperative qûmāh is an urgent call; the cohortative form adds emotional intensity. The archaic battle cry echoes Numbers 10:35 (the ark-procession cry: "Rise up, O LORD!"). שִׁבַּרְתָּ (shibbartā, "you shatter") — the perfect of confidence: not a future hope but a declaration of what God does, treating the anticipated rescue as accomplished. The jaw/teeth imagery (לֶחִי, cheek; שִׁנֵּי, teeth) disables the enemy's capacity to wound.
8: לַיהוָה הַיְשׁוּעָה (laYHWH hayyĕshûʿāh, "Salvation belongs to the LORD") — the noun with the definite article: the salvation, not a salvation. It is a possession of the LORD, not a human achievement or political strategy. This declaration closes the psalm as a theological assertion: whatever the enemies say (v2), they are wrong. Selāh closes the psalm as it marked the crisis of v2.
